title: Anger at the Friend Who Stopped Calling After You Lost the Baby
dek: When the silence of a friend echoes louder than the grief itself, your body registers the betrayal. This is not a failure of forgiveness; it is the biology of a nervous system demanding the loyalty it was promised.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body’s Quiet Grief: When Anger Rises at the Friend Who Disappeared

There’s a sharpness in your jaw, the masseter muscle clenched so tightly it’s a wonder you can even open your mouth to speak. The corners of your lips press down, pulling at the fascia around your cheeks, as if trying to hold a secret too fierce for words. It’s 2 a.m., and the night is swollen with the smell of damp earth and old cedar from the woods behind your house. You lie awake, the silence between your ribs as tight as the coil of your levator scapulae, stretched thin by the weight of your disappointment. The air is cool and still, but inside you, a storm brews—silent but relentless. This is the body’s way of telling you: you are furious. And you are allowed to be.

You lost a child. The friend who once called weekly, who knew the shape of your dreams and the color of your fears, has gone silent. No texts, no voicemails, no “how are you holding up?” The disappearance is not just a social slight—it is a rupture that echoes in the tendons of your neck and the hollow of your diaphragm. This anger is not a sign of moral failure, nor a character flaw. It is a messenger, raw and unfiltered, shouting across the empty spaces between you.

This article is for you—the woman who was taught that fury is ugly, that it must be smoothed over with smiles or silenced with apologies. Here, you will be met without shame, with the unflinching respect that your body demands. We will trace the tendrils of your anger through muscle and fascia, through the herbs that can steady your nervous system, and through the wisdom of teachers who understand that anger is not pathology but biology.

## The Jaw Remembers: Anatomy of Anger in the Body

When anger wells up, it often lodges first in the jaw. The masseter, responsible for clenching your teeth, tightens as if to guard a secret you are not yet ready to share. The temporalis muscles fan out across your temples, pulsing with tension. This is your fight response, your body’s way of bracing for confrontation, even if the conflict is internal, even if it is silent and unseen. You might catch yourself grinding your teeth late at night, the sound muffled by pillows, your tongue pressing hard against the roof of your mouth as if to contain the roar inside.

The trapezius muscle, spanning your shoulders and neck, contracts next. The levator scapulae pulls your shoulder blades up toward your ears, creating a rigid frame around your heart. You might notice the skin on your upper back feels prickly, almost alive, as if your nerves themselves are standing at attention. The small muscles between your shoulder blades—the rhomboids—ache with a dull, persistent tightness, as if the betrayal has been tattooed onto your back. This is not just anger; this is betrayal made flesh.

Your diaphragm, the great dome of muscle beneath your lungs, tightens in response. You might catch yourself holding your breath, the breath becoming shallow and quick, like the flutter of wings trapped behind glass. The breath is a barometer of your emotional state—when anger constricts the diaphragm, your body prepares to either fight or flee, but in this case, you are trapped in place, caught in the silence of a friendship that has fallen away. You might notice a hollow ache just beneath your sternum, a subtle pulling downward that feels like grief’s weight settling into your core.

This tension is no accident. As Pema Chödrön teaches, “When we resist or try to avoid our painful feelings, we only make them stronger.” The jaw tightens, the shoulders rise, the breath constricts—all these are your body’s way of saying, “Notice me. I am in pain.” The fascia that wraps around these muscles—the dense, fibrous tissue that connects every part of you—remembers this pain long after the moment has passed. It hardens around the betrayal, like sap thickening on old cedar, sticky and unyielding.

In the quiet hours, when the house exhales and the moonlight spills silver across your skin, your body replays these tensions like a somatic echo, reminding you that anger is a living thread woven into your very being.

## The Anger of Abandonment: Naming the Unspoken Wound

The friend who stopped calling did not merely lapse into silence. Their absence is a wound shaped like abandonment, a hollow where your trust once lived. This absence ignites a firestorm of anger, but its roots are tangled with sadness and disbelief. You might find yourself replaying conversations, trying to find the exact moment when everything shifted, when the connection frayed and finally broke. The memory may come with a sudden tightening in the psoas muscle, deep within your pelvis—like a clenched fist holding your core rigid, as if bracing you against further loss.

Karla McLaren, a pioneer in the emotional intelligence of anger, reminds us that anger is “a protective emotion that warns us when someone has crossed a boundary.” Your anger at this friend is not random bitterness; it is a signal that your boundaries, your need for support and recognition in your grief, were ignored. You are right to feel the sting of this betrayal. This anger is your body’s way of insisting on visibility—of demanding that your pain be acknowledged.

This anger is complicated. It is wrapped tightly with grief, and it demands a witness. But society often tells you that anger, especially in women, is unattractive or dangerous, particularly when it arises from loss. You were taught to smooth over your pain with pleasantries, to minimize your fury lest you be labeled “difficult” or “too much.” But the body remembers what the mind tries to forget. The psoas, sometimes called the “muscle of the soul,” is an ancient witness to your suffering. When it tightens, it can feel like a visceral knot in your lower belly—a physical manifestation of the rejection that cuts deeper than words.

Your nervous system responds as well. The sympathetic nervous system floods your body with adrenaline, priming your muscles for action even when no action is possible. You might notice your heart racing, your hands trembling, or an inexplicable heat rising to your face. Resmaa Menakem speaks often of how trauma lodges not just in the mind but in the body’s musculature—how the “blue spot” in the brain’s brainstem triggers a primal freeze response, sending signals to the muscles to stay tense, alert, vigilant. This is not weakness; this is survival.

The betrayal is not just emotional; it is embedded in your fascia, your connective tissue, which remembers trauma long after the mind has moved on. The fascia’s memory is silent but persistent, like the lingering scent of cedar after a storm. It hardens, contracts, and thickens around the betrayal, making movement difficult—not just physical movement, but the movement of your heart toward trust again.

## Herbs as Allies: Medicine for the Angry, Grieving Body

Your body’s chemistry is out of balance. The surge of adrenaline from your anger has left your heart racing, your hands clammy, and your stomach knotted. The nervous system is on high alert, the parasympathetic branch, which calms and restores, pushed into the background. Here, herbs become serious medicine, not mere wellness trends.

Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is a bitter, calming herb used in Traditional Chinese Medicine to soothe the heart and ease tension in the chest. It acts gently on the diaphragm and the vagus nerve, helping to regulate your breath and quiet the storm in your ribcage. A tincture of motherwort taken before bed can ease the tightness in your chest and soften the clenched muscles around your heart. The herb’s bitter taste is a medicine for the bitterness in your body, a reminder that healing sometimes requires holding discomfort rather than fleeing it.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is another ally for the nervous system. Known as a nervine relaxant, it helps release the tension in the trapezius and levator scapulae muscles. When your neck feels like a steel trap, a tea of skullcap steeps this tightness into a slow unraveling. Its subtle bitterness reminds us that healing is sometimes a slow burn, not a sudden extinguishing. The aroma of skullcap tea—earthy, with hints of mint and pine—can ground you in the moment, anchoring your scattered nerves.

If your anger leaves you restless, unable to find a moment’s peace even in sleep, consider Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera). This adaptogen modulates the body’s stress response, lowering cortisol levels and easing the tension in your psoas and diaphragm. Taken regularly, it can help restore balance to a body stuck in fight-or-flight mode. The warm, slightly sweet taste of Ashwagandha tincture holds a quiet power, like a hearth fire on a cold night, inviting your muscles to soften and your breath to deepen.

For a tincture blend, consider motherwort and skullcap in equal parts, with a pinch of lavender flower for its calming aroma. Take 30 drops in water in the early evening as the sky turns indigo and the first stars blink awake. This is a medicine ritual, a way of telling your body that it is safe to relax, that your anger is honored but not allowed to consume you. The lavender’s floral scent weaves into the bitterness, softening the edges of your emotional pain, grounding you in the present moment.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, these herbs are understood not just as chemical compounds but as energetic allies. Motherwort moves stagnant Liver Qi, often stuck in the chest and shoulders during emotional upheaval. Skullcap calms the Shen—the spirit—and quiets the mind’s restless chatter. Ashwagandha nourishes the Kidney Yin, supporting resilience to chronic stress. Together, they form a triad of support for a body and heart in turmoil.

## The Wisdom of Thich Nhat Hanh: Embracing Anger with Compassion

The Vietnamese Zen teacher Thich Nhat Hanh offers a radical approach to anger: to hold it gently, as you would a wounded child. He teaches that anger is not the enemy, but a messenger carrying a precious insight. When you feel that tightness in your masseter, that heavy weight in your trapezius, you can breathe into those places and say, “I see you. I am here.”

His practice of mindful breathing is not about suppressing anger but about transforming it. When your friend’s silence feels like a betrayal, try this: place your hand on your belly and breathe deeply into the diaphragm, feeling it expand like a bellows moving air into a dying fire. Breathe in the anger; breathe out the pain. You are not your anger, but you are its home.

Thich Nhat Hanh invites you to say silently with each breath, “Breathing in, I know anger is here. Breathing out, I release my desire to hurt.” This practice softens the muscle tension and reminds you that anger can be a path to understanding, a teacher rather than a tyrant. The breath becomes a bridge from the clenched masseter to the softening belly, from the rigid trapezius to the surrender of the levator scapulae. You become the space in which anger can move without destroying.

His teachings remind us that anger, like all emotions, wants to be seen and heard—not acted out, but acknowledged. The practice is not about fixing or erasing anger, but about giving it room to breathe, to exist as part of your living body. In the stillness of your breath, you may find a flicker of tenderness toward the friend who left, the grief that underpins your fury, and ultimately, toward yourself.

## Robert Augustus Masters on Shame and Anger: Holding the Complexity

Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic therapist who works deeply with shame and trauma, insists that anger and shame are often two sides of the same coin. You may feel shame beneath your fury—the shame of being abandoned, the shame of grieving a loss that others don’t acknowledge or understand.

Masters teaches that when we allow ourselves to feel our anger fully, without judgment, we also begin to see the shame that lurks beneath it. This is not about self-flagellation but about somatic witnessing—feeling into the tension in your masseter, the tightness in your diaphragm, the trembling in your hands, and saying, “I am here with you, all of you.”

This is radical self-compassion, deeply embodied. Your anger is loud, but your shame is quieter, nestled in the psoas, holding your pelvis rigid and immobile. When you breathe into these muscles, you begin to soften the shame’s grip, allowing it to release. The shame is a slow, low heat, like embers glowing beneath cold ashes. It does not burn fiercely but weighs heavy, pressing down on your lower back and hips.

Masters encourages a practice of “grounded witnessing.” Sit with your anger as if it were a person in the room. Notice the muscle tension, the heat in your face, the quickening of your pulse. Then, without trying to change anything, simply stay present with it, breathing into the muscles that hold the story. The body knows what to do when it is seen.

This witnessing is a kind of radical hospitality for your inner world. It requires you to lean into discomfort, to hold paradox—the simultaneous presence of rage and tenderness, betrayal and longing. Your body is your most honest companion in this work, its sensations a map through the landscape of your grief.

## An Embodied Practice: Breathing into the Jaw and Shoulders

Right now, find a place where you can sit comfortably, your feet resting firmly on the ground. Place your right hand lightly over your masseter muscle, the muscle you use to clench your jaw. Feel the hardness beneath your palm, the way your teeth press together just beneath the surface. Notice the subtle pulse of tension, the way it contracts in response to old hurts.

Take a slow, deliberate breath in through your nose, counting to four. Feel your diaphragm expand, your belly rising under your hand. Hold the breath for a moment, then exhale slowly through your mouth, imagining the tension in your jaw melting like wax. Repeat this three times.

Next, place your left hand over your trapezius muscle, the thick band of muscle that runs from your neck to your shoulder. As you breathe in, imagine sending warmth and softness into this muscle, loosening the tightness. As you breathe out, feel the weight of your shoulders dropping, the levator scapulae releasing the tension that holds your ears close to your shoulders.

With your hands still resting gently, allow your breath to deepen, inviting the psoas to relax by imagining your pelvis softening, your lower back lengthening. You might visualize the fascia unwinding like a spool of thread, releasing decades of stored tension. Stay here for several minutes. The anger is present, but so is your capacity to hold it with care.

You may notice subtle shifts—perhaps a small tremor in your hands or a softening around your eyes. These are signs your body is beginning to let go, to move from contraction to expansion. This practice is not about erasing anger but about creating space for it to exist without overwhelming you.

## The Long Night of Grief: Honoring Your Body’s Timeline

Anger at the friend who stopped calling will not disappear overnight, nor should it. The body’s timeline for grief is different from the mind’s. You might find that the tightness in your masseter loosens slightly, only to flare again weeks or months later, triggered by a memory, a song, a scent. This is not a regression; it is the natural rhythm of healing. The body processes trauma in layers, unwinding the fascia slowly, honoring the depth of the wound.

When the anger returns, greet it as an old friend. Notice where it lives in your body. Is it in the jaw? The shoulders? The diaphragm? Breathe into those spaces. Offer them the medicine of your attention, the bitter comfort of motherwort, the quiet wisdom of Thich Nhat Hanh. You are not failing at forgiveness; you are succeeding at feeling.

The friend who left may never understand the depth of your pain, but your body does. Your body is your most faithful witness, holding the truth of your experience when others turn away. By honoring your anger, you are honoring the child you lost, the love you still carry, and the fierce, unyielding strength of your own heart.

## FAQ: Navigating Anger and Abandonment

**Q: Is it normal to feel this angry at a friend who disappeared after my loss?**
**A:** Yes. Your anger is a biological response to a ruptured boundary. When someone you trusted fails to show up during a profound crisis, your nervous system registers it as a threat to your safety and connection. This anger is not a moral failing; it is your body’s way of demanding the support you deserved.

**Q: How can I stop clenching my jaw when I think about this friend?**
**A:** The masseter muscle holds the tension of unspoken words. Instead of trying to force it to relax, try the embodied practice of placing your hand over the muscle and breathing into it. Acknowledge the tension. You might also try a nervine herb like skullcap to help soothe the nervous system and release the physical grip of the anger.

**Q: What if I never get an apology from them?**
**A:** The body’s healing does not depend on an external apology. While an apology can be validating, your healing comes from witnessing your own pain. By honoring your anger and tending to your nervous system, you create the safety and validation within yourself that the friend failed to provide.

**Q: Are there specific herbs that can help with the physical symptoms of this anger?**
**A:** Yes. Motherwort is excellent for easing tension in the chest and regulating the heart rate. Skullcap helps release tension in the neck and shoulders, while Ashwagandha can support the body in moving out of a chronic fight-or-flight state. Always consult with a qualified herbalist or healthcare provider before starting new herbs.

**Q: How do I know if my anger is becoming unhealthy?**
**A:** Anger becomes unhealthy when it turns inward as shame or outward as destructive behavior. If your anger feels like a trap you cannot escape, or if it is causing chronic physical pain or disrupting your ability to function, it may be helpful to seek the support of a somatic therapist who can help you process the trauma stored in your body.
